message:
chore(beads): sync issues.jsonl — additional follow-up state from parallel agents

Routine state sync. Captures issue updates that landed in the local
SQLite store while parallel agents kept working through the test-
pinning backlog. Same shape as the earlier sync commit in this push:
no schema changes, no in-place edits to issue bodies, just status
transitions and any new rows queued for the swarm.

Keeps `.beads/issues.jsonl` aligned with the local SQLite store so
other agents pulling main get a consistent view.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gqvh","title":"[FCP2] Rate Limiting: ThrottleViolation, Backpressure Signals, Quota Enforcement","description":"# [FCP2] Rate Limiting: ThrottleViolation, Backpressure Signals, Quota Enforcement

## Goal
Implement rate limiting at multiple layers: per-connector, per-operation, per-zone, and mesh-wide.

## Why This Matters
Without rate limiting:
- A runaway connector can exhaust external API quotas
- A compromised node can DoS the mesh
- Resource exhaustion becomes undetectable until failure

## Rate Limit Layers

### Layer 1: Connector Manifest Limits
Declared in connector manifest:
```toml
[rate_limits]
requests_per_minute = 60
requests_per_hour = 1000
concurrent_operations = 5
```

### Layer 2: Zone Policy Limits
```
ZonePolicy.rate_limits {
  per_connector_rpm: u32,
  per_zone_rpm: u32,
  burst_allowance: f32,  // multiplier for burst
}
```

### Layer 3: Capability Limits
Capabilities can include rate constraints:
```
CapabilityGrant.constraints.rate_limit {
  max_per_minute: 10,
  max_concurrent: 2,
}
```

### Layer 4: MeshNode Admission Control
Global limits per peer node (covered in admission control bead).

## ThrottleViolation Object
When rate limit is exceeded:
```
ThrottleViolation {
  violation_id: ObjectId,
  timestamp: u64,
  zone_id: ZoneId,
  connector_id: Option<ConnectorId>,
  operation_id: Option<SchemaId>,
  limit_type: LimitType,  // RPM, Concurrent, Burst, Quota
  limit_value: u32,
  current_value: u32,
  retry_after_ms: u64,
}
```

ThrottleViolation is:
- Recorded in audit chain
- Returned in InvokeResponse error
- Triggers backpressure signals

## Backpressure Protocol
When approaching limits:
1. **Warning Zone (80%)**: Emit metric, log warning
2. **Soft Limit (95%)**: Return `retry_after_ms` in responses
3. **Hard Limit (100%)**: Reject with FCP-4290 (RateLimited)

For streaming operations:
- Flow control via FCPC backpressure frames
- Connector can request pause/resume

## Token Bucket Implementation
Use token bucket algorithm with:
- `capacity`: max burst size
- `refill_rate`: tokens per second
- `current_tokens`: available tokens

Tokens are consumed per-operation based on:
- Base cost (1 token)
- Size cost (for data transfer)
- Compute cost (for expensive operations)

## Tests
- Unit tests: token bucket refill and consumption
- Property tests: rate limit never exceeds declared max
- Integration tests: concurrent requests properly limited
- Adversarial tests: burst exhaustion followed by gradual recovery
- E2E tests: ThrottleViolation appears in audit chain

## Acceptance Criteria
- Rate limits are enforced at all layers
- ThrottleViolation objects are recorded for audit
- Backpressure signals enable graceful degradation

","notes":"Progress: implemented core rate-limit artifacts + enforcement primitives.

- fcp-core: added ThrottleViolation (+ ThrottleViolationInput), LimitType, BackpressureLevel/Signal; extended FcpError::RateLimited to carry optional boxed ThrottleViolation and include it in structured error details.
- fcp-ratelimit: added FCP glue (config_from_core, enforce() -> EnforcementOutcome w/ backpressure + violation); token cost model (base + bytes ceil-div + compute); ConcurrencyLimiter with try_acquire_or_violation(); RateLimiter::try_acquire_n and atomic multi-permit TokenBucket consumption.
- Tests: new unit tests for backpressure thresholds, violation propagation into FcpErrorResponse.details, token cost, concurrency limiter, and atomic multi-permit consumption.
- Quality gates: cargo fmt/check/clippy/test all green.

Remaining (blocked by other beads): integrate with policy engine + audit chain storage, and mesh-wide admission control + E2E scenarios.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"Dicklesworthstone","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T07:53:39.479087624Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T17:45:17.444690654Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T17:45:17.444690654Z","close_reason":"Core rate-limit artifacts + enforcement primitives complete: ThrottleViolation, BackpressureLevel/Signal, TokenBucket, ConcurrencyLimiter, FCP glue (config_from_core, enforce).  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gr1","title":"fcp.hubspot: Pipeline analytics and reporting","description":"# fcp.hubspot: Pipeline analytics and reporting (FCP2)

## Goal
Expose HubSpot reporting surfaces needed for:
- pipeline health dashboards
- stage conversion analysis
- activity/velocity metrics

This is primarily read-only but may include access to sensitive business data.

## Scope
### Read
- Retrieve pipeline definitions (stages, probabilities).
- Fetch metrics:
  - counts by stage
  - conversion rates
  - time-in-stage
  - win/loss trends
- Support time window filters and stable pagination.

### Output shaping
- Prefer aggregate metrics and IDs over raw deal details where possible.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `hubspot.analytics.read`
- `hubspot.pipelines.read`

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- Avoid logging raw deal contents.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `hubspot.pipeline.list`
  - `hubspot.pipeline.metrics`
  - `hubspot.pipeline.stage_metrics`
- Consistent metric naming + units.

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - time window handling
  - pagination
  - error mapping

## Acceptance criteria
- Agents can answer “how is the pipeline doing?” without exposing raw CRM PII in logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-ky3yv","title":"[MOR/C3.1] Implement phantom types for Verified/Unverified CapabilityTokens","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:34:54.783764Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-10T22:36:18.366988Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T22:36:08.158117Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["P0-critical","crypto","security","type-safety"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ky3yv","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-8whb3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:37:04.543072Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2640,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ky3yv","author":"jemanuel","text":"WHAT: Introduce phantom type markers Unverified and Verified on CapabilityToken<State>. After deserialization, tokens are CapabilityToken<Unverified> — they CANNOT have their claims accessed. Only after calling verify(key) which returns CapabilityToken<Verified> can claims be read. Functions that accept capability operations (invoke, simulate, etc.) require CapabilityToken<Verified>. WHY: A7 Type-Theoretic found that token verification is OPTIONAL in the current API. A programmer can deserialize a token and use its claims without ever calling verify(). H2 Adversarial found holder-binding bypass and clock-skew attacks that succeed when verification is skipped. The phantom type pattern makes it IMPOSSIBLE to use unverified claims — the compiler rejects it. IMPLEMENTATION SKETCH: ```rust struct Unverified; struct Verified; pub struct CapabilityToken<State = Unverified> { raw: CoseToken, _phantom: PhantomData<State>, } impl CapabilityToken<Unverified> { pub fn deserialize(bytes: &[u8]) -> Result<Self, Error> { ... } pub fn verify(self, key: &Ed25519VerifyingKey) -> Result<CapabilityToken<Verified>, Error> { // Consuming method — Unverified token is gone after this // Verify signature, expiry, revocation, holder binding Ok(CapabilityToken { raw: self.raw, _phantom: PhantomData }) } } impl CapabilityToken<Verified> { pub fn capability_id(&self) -> &str { ... } pub fn zone_id(&self) -> &ZoneId { ... } pub fn constraints(&self) -> &CapabilityConstraints { ... } } // Functions requiring verified tokens: async fn invoke(token: &CapabilityToken<Verified>, req: InvokeRequest) -> ... ``` WHERE: crates/fcp-core/src/capability.rs — CapabilityToken struct. crates/fcp-crypto/src/cose.rs — CoseToken. crates/fcp-sdk/src/lib.rs — FcpConnector trait (invoke signature). All callers that currently access token claims must be updated. BLAST RADIUS: This is a breaking change to the CapabilityToken API. All code that deserializes tokens must now call verify() before accessing claims. Estimate 30-50 call sites across platform crates and connectors. ACCEPTANCE: (1) CapabilityToken<Unverified> has NO methods exposing claims. (2) verify() is a consuming method returning CapabilityToken<Verified>. (3) All claim-accessing methods only on CapabilityToken<Verified>. (4) invoke/simulate/introspect signatures require Verified. (5) Compilation fails if any code path uses unverified claims. (6) At least 8 tests: deserialize-then-verify happy path, deserialize-without-verify compile error (doc test), expired token rejected by verify, revoked token rejected by verify, holder mismatch rejected, clock-skew tolerance works, serialization round-trip, verify-then-access-claims. ESTIMATED EFFORT: 3-4 days (type change + 30-50 call sites + tests). DESIGN NOTE: The consuming verify() method means the Unverified token is MOVED into the Verified token. The original cannot be reused. This prevents use-before-verify and double-verify patterns. The PhantomData<State> adds zero runtime cost — it is erased at compilation.","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:36:47Z"},{"id":2654,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ky3yv","author":"jemanuel","text":"COMPLETED by SunnyMoose (commit d090e532). Changes: (1) verify() now consumes CapabilityToken (owned, not &ref) - prevents use-before-verify and double-verify at compile time. (2) verify_claims() kept as non-consuming reference variant. (3) Polling/Webhook trait signatures require CapabilityToken<Verified>. (4) policy.rs: documented intentional unverified access in simulate_policy_decision. (5) 17 phantom type acceptance tests (7 new). (6) Updated ~93 call sites across connectors, E2E tests, platform crates. All 3905 lib tests + 82 golden vectors pass, clippy clean.","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:35:03Z"},{"id":2655,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ky3yv","author":"jemanuel","text":"SunnyMoose: C3.1 phantom types complete. verify() now takes owned CapabilityToken (consuming), returns CapabilityToken<Verified>. verify_claims() remains by-reference. Commits: 638175f9, d090e532, 373dbc49.","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:35:59Z"},{"id":2656,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-ky3yv","author":"jemanuel","text":"C3.1 Phantom Types for CapabilityToken — IMPLEMENTED. Added Unverified/Verified phantom type markers to CapabilityToken<S>. Private raw field with raw()/into_raw() accessors. verify() consumes Unverified → Verified. verify_claims() is non-consuming ref variant. claims() only on Verified. Commits: d090e532, 373dbc49.","created_at":"2026-04-10T22:36:18Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-nvt","title":"fcp.sentry: Issue assignment and status management","description":"# fcp.sentry: Issue assignment and status management (FCP2)

## Goal
Allow safe, auditable manipulation of Sentry issue workflow state:
- assign/unassign
- resolve/unresolve
- ignore/unignore
- set priority (if supported)

These are write operations that can affect on-call workflows.

## Scope
### Write operations
- `sentry.issue.assign`
- `sentry.issue.resolve`
- `sentry.issue.ignore`
- `sentry.issue.update_priority`

### Read support
- fetch minimal issue metadata required for optimistic concurrency (if available).

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `sentry.issues.write`

Policy guidance:
- Some orgs may require approvals for resolving/ignoring issues.

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- For every write:
  - emit OperationReceipt + AuditEvent
  - include issue id + action + correlation id
  - never include raw issue payloads in logs

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `sentry.issue.assign`
  - `sentry.issue.set_status` (resolve/ignore/unresolve)
  - `sentry.issue.set_priority`
- Denial reason codes for policy engine.

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - capability gating
  - approval gating (when policy requires)
  - error mapping (permission denied, conflict)
  - audit/receipt emission

## Acceptance criteria
- Issue workflow actions are safe, explainable, and auditable.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-o1na6","title":"[PERF] fcp-bootstrap::open_authenticated_session: global PKCS#11 lock serializes all sessions","description":"crates/fcp-bootstrap/src/hardware_token.rs:451-503 and 585-597. open_authenticated_session() grabs provider_probe_lock() before PKCS#11 initialize/open/login and keeps that MutexGuard alive inside the session close closure until the caller eventually closes the authenticated session. Enumeration sessions do the same for their whole lifetime. That turns hardware-token rotation, cert enumeration, and key enumeration into a process-wide serialized critical section: one long-lived session blocks unrelated providers/slots from even starting, so throughput collapses under parallel bootstrap or rotation work. Proposed improvement: narrow the lock to the init/finalize transition or make it provider-scoped/refcounted instead of holding one global mutex for the full lifetime of every authenticated or enumeration session.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:56:28.975339Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T21:46:18.096552Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T21:46:18.096214Z","close_reason":"PKCS#11 session opens now use provider-scoped active-session accounting so init/finalize stay serialized without holding the global lock for the full authenticated session lifetime","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

(d) finite values round-trip via to_canonical_cbor -> from_reader

arbitrary_leaf branch substitutes 0.0 when is_nan), so the rejection
branch is uncovered.

Properties: (1) panic-free over arbitrary u64 reinterpreted as f64;
(2) NaN/Inf rejection over signaling/quiet NaN payloads + +-Infinity
bit patterns; (3) -0.0 canonical zero - encode(-0.0) byte-equals

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-w82c","title":"[FCP2] Long-Running Operation Progress: Real-Time Feedback","description":"# [FCP2] Long-Running Operation Progress: Real-Time Feedback

## Goal
Enable agents to receive progress updates for long-running operations without blocking or polling.

## Problem
Some connector operations take significant time:
- Large file uploads/downloads
- Batch API calls
- Data migrations
- Complex queries

Agents currently have no visibility into progress - they either block waiting or timeout without knowing how close the operation was to completion.

## Solution: Progress Streaming

### Request Format
```jsonc
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "method": "tools/invoke",
  "params": {
    "tool": "fcp.storage.upload",
    "input": {
      "path": "/large-file.tar.gz",
      "destination": "s3://bucket/path"
    },
    "options": {
      "stream_progress": true,  // Request progress updates
      "progress_interval_ms": 500  // Min interval between updates
    }
  }
}
```

### Progress Notification Format
When `stream_progress: true`, the host sends JSON-RPC notifications:

```jsonc
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "method": "progress/update",
  "params": {
    "operation_id": "op_abc123",
    "request_id": 1,  // Correlates to original request
    "progress": {
      "phase": "uploading",  // Current phase name
      "current": 45000000,   // Current progress (bytes, items, etc.)
      "total": 100000000,    // Total expected (if known)
      "unit": "bytes",       // Unit of measurement
      "percentage": 45.0,    // Computed percentage
      "rate": 5000000,       // Rate per second (if applicable)
      "eta_ms": 11000,       // Estimated time remaining
      "message": "Uploading chunk 9 of 20"  // Human-readable status
    },
    "timestamp": "2026-01-22T18:00:00Z"
  }
}
```

### Phase Transitions
```jsonc
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "method": "progress/phase",
  "params": {
    "operation_id": "op_abc123",
    "request_id": 1,
    "from_phase": "preparing",
    "to_phase": "uploading",
    "phases_remaining": ["verifying", "completing"],
    "timestamp": "2026-01-22T18:00:00Z"
  }
}
```

### Implementation

```rust
pub struct ProgressTracker {
    operation_id: OperationId,
    request_id: RequestId,
    sender: mpsc::Sender<ProgressNotification>,
    interval: Duration,
    last_sent: Instant,
}

impl ProgressTracker {
    /// Report progress (throttled by interval)
    pub async fn report(&mut self, progress: Progress) -> Result<(), FcpError> {
        if self.last_sent.elapsed() >= self.interval {
            let notification = ProgressNotification {
                operation_id: self.operation_id.clone(),
                request_id: self.request_id,
                progress,
                timestamp: Utc::now(),
            };
            self.sender.send(notification).await?;
            self.last_sent = Instant::now();
        }
        Ok(())
    }

    /// Report phase transition (always sent)
    pub async fn transition(&mut self, from: &str, to: &str, remaining: &[&str]) -> Result<(), FcpError> {
        let notification = PhaseTransition {
            operation_id: self.operation_id.clone(),
            request_id: self.request_id,
            from_phase: from.to_string(),
            to_phase: to.to_string(),
            phases_remaining: remaining.iter().map(|s| s.to_string()).collect(),
            timestamp: Utc::now(),
        };
        self.sender.send(notification.into()).await?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

pub struct Progress {
    pub phase: String,
    pub current: u64,
    pub total: Option<u64>,
    pub unit: ProgressUnit,
    pub rate: Option<u64>,
    pub message: Option<String>,
}

pub enum ProgressUnit {
    Bytes,
    Items,
    Requests,
    Rows,
    Custom(String),
}
```

### Connector SDK Support

```rust
#[fcp_tool(streaming = true)]
async fn upload_file(
    ctx: &ToolContext,
    input: UploadInput,
    progress: ProgressTracker,  // Injected if stream_progress=true
) -> Result<UploadOutput, FcpError> {
    progress.transition("preparing", "uploading", &["verifying"]).await?;

    let mut uploaded = 0u64;
    for chunk in file.chunks(CHUNK_SIZE) {
        upload_chunk(chunk).await?;
        uploaded += chunk.len() as u64;
        progress.report(Progress {
            phase: "uploading".to_string(),
            current: uploaded,
            total: Some(file_size),
            unit: ProgressUnit::Bytes,
            rate: Some(calculate_rate()),
            message: Some(format!("Uploaded {} of {}", uploaded, file_size)),
        }).await?;
    }

    progress.transition("uploading", "verifying", &[]).await?;
    verify_upload().await?;

    Ok(UploadOutput { bytes_uploaded: uploaded })
}
```

### Tests

```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_progress_streaming_basic() {
    let harness = TestHarness::new().await;
    let (tx, mut rx) = mpsc::channel(100);

    harness.invoke_with_progress("test.slow_operation", json!({}), tx).await;

    let mut progress_updates = vec![];
    while let Some(update) = rx.recv().await {
        progress_updates.push(update);
    }

    assert!(progress_updates.len() > 0);
    // Progress should be monotonically increasing
    for window in progress_updates.windows(2) {
        assert!(window[1].progress.percentage >= window[0].progress.percentage);
    }
}

#[tokio::test]
async fn test_progress_interval_throttling() {
    // Progress updates respect interval setting
}

#[tokio::test]

    // Phase transitions are always sent
}

#[tokio::test]

    // Works when total is unknown (indeterminate)

#[tokio::test]

    // Progress stops when operation is cancelled
}
```

- [ ] Phase transitions are always sent (not throttled)
- [ ] Progress includes rate and ETA when calculable
- [ ] Works with both deterministic and indeterminate progress
- [ ] Progress stops immediately on cancellation

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-wsbgg","title":"[CONFORMANCE-GAP] capability: missing nbf/exp boundary coverage at issuance + verification","description":"Surface: capability-token issuance + verification timing.

Background: capability tokens carry not_before (nbf) and expiration (exp) claims (auth_claims_conformance.rs:114-115). Verification logic is supposed to:
- Reject before nbf (not-yet-valid)
- Accept at nbf exactly
- Accept up to but excluding exp
- Reject at exp exactly (RFC 7519 §4.1.4: MUST NOT be processed on or after)
- Reject after exp

Gap: crates/fcp-conformance/tests/auth_claims_conformance.rs uses far-future exp (timestamp 2_000_000_000+) and far-past nbf, so the at-boundary semantics are never exercised. A grep for `fn .*nbf` / `fn .*expir` / `fn .*not_before` / `fn .*at_boundary` / `fn .*just_before` / `fn .*just_after` across crates/fcp-conformance/tests/auth_claims_conformance.rs and crates/fcp-conformance/src/vectors/capability.rs returns zero hits. There is no boundary-test that pins whether the verifier uses inclusive or exclusive comparison for either bound.

Suggested test scenarios (add to auth_claims_conformance.rs OR new tests/capability_token_timing_conformance.rs):

1. Token with nbf equal to now exactly: MUST verify (issuance moment is valid).
2. Token with nbf one second in the future: MUST reject (not_yet_valid).
3. Token with exp equal to now exactly: MUST reject per RFC 7519 (expired-at-exp boundary).
4. Token with exp one second in the future: MUST verify.
5. Token with nbf greater than exp (logically impossible): MUST reject as malformed claim set.
6. Token with no exp claim: MUST reject (FCP requires bounded validity per spec) OR accept with warning — current behavior should be locked in either way.
7. nbf identical to exp (zero-duration token): MUST reject (no valid window).

Why P2: timing-boundary semantics are subtle and easy to drift on a refactor. RFC 7519 has explicit MUST language; the conformance suite should mirror it.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-26T01:24:06.863098Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T01:42:00.885807Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T01:42:00.878574Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2902,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-wsbgg","author":"jemanuel","text":"Shipped in commit bb03ae4f: new crates/fcp-conformance/tests/capability_token_timing_conformance.rs (253 lines, 9 tests) pinning nbf/exp boundary semantics around CAPABILITY_TOKEN_CLOCK_SKEW_SECS (300s).  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-xedeu","title":"TEST-FUZZ-X: NodeKeyAttestation sign/verify field-binding + tag canonicalization fuzz","description":"fcp_tailscale::NodeKeyAttestation::sign + verify (identity.rs:302-387)
is the owner-signed attestation that binds (node_id, signing_kid,
encryption_kid, issuance_kid, tags, issued_at, expires_at) under an
Ed25519 signature. The mesh's identity model relies on this binding —
fcp_tags() and verified_fcp_tags() (identity.rs:227-246) refuse to
emit a node's zone memberships unless the attestation verifies, so any
binding-regression in sign/verify lets an attacker spoof zone tags or
swap a key for one they control.

Existing fuzz coverage in fcp-tailscale:
  - tailscale_acl: ZoneTagMapping zone↔tag round-trip + TailscaleTag::new
  - tailscale_status_parse: TailscaleStatus JSON deserialization
    + peers() consistency

NOT covered: the attestation sign/verify surface. Properties a regression
would silently break:

  1. **Round-trip**: sign(...) → verify(...) succeeds for the same
     (node_id, keys, tags, owner) tuple with sufficient validity_hours.
  2. **Tag canonicalization**: canonical_tag_strings (identity.rs:266-
     271) sort_unstable + dedup before signing — so signing with
     tags=[A,B,C] MUST verify under tags=[C,B,A] (sort) AND under
     tags=[A,A,B,C] (dedup). A regression that dropped the sort or
     dedup would let an attacker swap a captured attestation's tag
     ORDER (or insert duplicates) and pass through verify.
  3. **Node-id binding**: verify with a different node_id MUST yield
     InvalidAttestation — guards against pivoting a captured
     attestation across nodes.
  4. **Key-set binding (×3)**: verify with a NodeKeys whose
     signing_kid, encryption_kid, OR issuance_kid differs from the
     sign-time keys MUST yield InvalidAttestation. A regression that
     dropped any of the three from the canonical signing bytes lets
